name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٥٣)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Take recourse in patience and prayer; indeed Allah is with the patient. (153)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتٌۢ ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ (١٥٤)
```

```english
Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware. (154)
```

```arabic
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُم بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلْخَوْفِ وَٱلْجُوعِ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَنفُسِ وَٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٥٥)
```

```english
We will surely test you with a measure of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth, lives, and fruits; and give good news to the patient (155)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّآ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ (١٥٦)
```

```english
—those who, when an affliction visits them, say, ‘Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return.’ (156)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَٰتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُهْتَدُونَ (١٥٧)
```

```english
It is they who receive the blessings of their Lord and [His] mercy, and it is they who are the [rightly] guided. (157)
```

## (Commentary)

These are five verses united in context (السياق), harmonious in their phrasing, and cohesive in their meanings. The first leads to the last, and the last refers back to the first. This reveals that they were revealed all at once, not separately. Their context proclaims that they were revealed shortly before the command to fight and the legislation of the ruling of struggle (الجهاد). They contain a mention of a trial (بلاء) that would soon face the believers and a calamity (مصيبة) that would strike them—and not just any trial or calamity, but a general trial that is not of ordinary occurrence but rather continuous in its happening. Indeed, the human species, like all other species existing in this natural plane of existence (النشأة الطبيعية), is not free in its individuals from particular events (الحادثة الجزئية) that disrupt the order of the individual in his personal life: such as death, illness, fear, hunger, grief, and deprivation. This is the sunnah (السنة) — *the established way and law* — of Allah that has proceeded among His servants and His creation. For this abode is the abode of mutual interference (التزاحم), and this plane of existence is the plane of change and transformation; and you will never find any change in the sunnah of Allah, nor will you ever find any alteration in the sunnah of Allah.

An individual trial, though difficult for the person afflicted by it and disliked, is not as terrifying or awe-inspiring as those general trials and tribulations. An individual draws strength for his intellect, resolve, and the steadfastness of his soul from the strengths of other individuals. As for general and all-encompassing trials, they strip away the collective consciousness (الشعور العمومي) and the totality of opinion, prudence, and management from the social body. The system of life is disrupted for them; fear is multiplied, alienation (الوحشة) accumulates, and the intellect and consciousness become agitated, while resolve and steadfastness fail. Thus, the general trial and the all-encompassing tribulation are more difficult and bitter, and it is this which the verses allude to.

Nor is it just any general trial like a plague or famine, but rather a general trial to which they brought themselves near. For they had embraced the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and answered the call of truth (دعوة الحق), while the world, and specifically their own people, opposed them in it. These opponents had no aim but to extinguish the light of Allah, uproot the word of justice, and nullify the call of truth. There was no means to sever the root of the dispute and end the disagreement other than fighting (القتال). All other means, such as establishing proof, spreading discord, or casting whispers and doubt, had become sterile and unproductive. The proof was with the Prophet, and the whispering, discord, and intrigue did not produce an effect that would satisfy the enemies of the religion. Thus, they had no means left but fighting and using it to block the path of truth and extinguish the brilliant, shining light of the religion. This was from the side of disbelief (الكفر); and the matter from the side of the religion was even clearer: there was no way to spread the word of monotheism, disseminate the true religion and the rule of justice, and uproot falsehood (الباطل) except through fighting. Indeed, the experiences extending from the time man descended into this abode demonstrate that truth only takes effect when falsehood is removed, and it will never be removed except by some exercise of power and force.

In short, there is an allusion in the verses to the approach of this tribulation by mentioning killing in the way of Allah, describing it with a description that leaves no disliked aspect or evil quality within it. It is that it is not death, but life—and what a life!

Thus, the verses rouse the believers to fight and inform them that before them is a trial and tribulation; they shall not attain the stations of excellence, the blessings of their Lord and His mercy, and guidance through His leading (الاهتداء بهدايته) except by being patient (الصبر) through it and enduring its hardships. He teaches them what to seek help from in facing it, which is patience and prayer (الصلوة). As for patience: it alone is the protection against anxiety (الجزع) and the disruption of management. As for prayer: it is a turning toward the Lord and a total devotion (الانقطاع) to Him in Whose hand is the command, and the realization that all power belongs to Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ** — "O you who have faith! Take recourse in patience and prayer; indeed Allah is with the patient" (2:153). A summary of the discussion on patience and prayer has already preceded in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying: **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ ۚ وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى الْخَاشِعِينَ** — "And take recourse in patience and prayer, and it is indeed hard except for the humble" (2:45). Patience (الصبر) is among the greatest of traits (الملكات) and states that the Quran praises, and it repeats the command for it until it reaches nearly seventy places in the Quran, such that it is said regarding it: **إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** — "that is indeed the steadiest of courses" (31:17). And it is said: **وَمَا يُلَقَّاهَا إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَمَا يُلَقَّاهَا إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ** — "But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except the greatly endowed" (41:35). And it is said: **إِنَّمَا يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُم بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ** — "Indeed the patient will be paid their reward in full without any reckoning" (39:10).

Prayer (الصلوة) is among the greatest acts of worship (العبادات) urged in the Quran, such that it is said regarding it: **إِنَّ الصَّلَاةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ الْفَحْشَاءِ وَالْمُنكَرِ** — "Indeed prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs" (29:45). Allah has not given any exhortations in His Book except that prayer was their head and their beginning.

Then He, the Glorified, described patience by saying that Allah is with the patient (الصابرين) who are characterized by patience. He did not describe prayer in this way, as He did in His saying: "And take recourse in patience and prayer, and it is indeed hard..." because the context in these verses is the context of facing terrors and clashing with champions. Thus, the emphasis on the matter of patience is more appropriate, unlike the previous verse. Therefore, it was said: "Indeed Allah is with the patient." This "withness" (المعية) is different from the withness indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ** — "and He is with you wherever you may be" (57:4). For that is the withness of all-encompassing presence (الإحاطة) and sustaining guardianship (القيمومة), unlike the withness with the patient, which is a withness of assistance (إعانة); thus, patience is the key to relief.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَا تَقُولُوا لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتٌ ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ** — "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware" (2:154). It might be said: The address is to the believers who have believed in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, and have submitted to the life of the hereafter (الحيوة الاخرة). It is inconceivable for them to say that a human being is nullified by death after they have answered the call of truth and heard many verses speaking of the Resurrection (المعاد). Furthermore, the verse only establishes life after death for a specific group, namely the martyrs (الشهداء) killed in the way of Allah, in contrast to other believers and all the disbelievers, even though the ruling of life after death is general and encompasses everyone. Therefore, what is meant by "life" is the survival of the name and beautiful remembrance throughout the ages. A group of commentators (المفسرين) has interpreted it in this way.

This is refuted **firstly:** by the fact that this being "life" exists only in the imagination (الوهم) and not in external reality (الخارج). It would be an imaginary life that has nothing in reality but the name. Such an illusory subject is not worthy of His speech, for He, the Exalted, calls to the truth and says: **فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلَالُ** — "So what is there after the truth except error?" (10:32). As for what Ibrahim asked for in his saying: **وَاجْعَل لِّي لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ** — "and confer on me a worthy repute among the posterity" (26:84), he only intended by it the survival of his true call and his truthful tongue after him, not merely good praise and beautiful remembrance.

Indeed, this false saying and deceptive illusion are only suitable for the condition of materialists (الماديين) and naturalists. They believed in the materiality of souls and their nullification upon death, and they denied the life of the hereafter. Then they sensed the human being's innate need, by primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — to speak of the survival of souls and their being affected by happiness and misery after death in noble matters, which cannot be attained without sacrifice and self-offering. This is especially true in great resolves where some people die and are killed so that others may live. If everyone who died was simply gone (فات), there would be no motive for a human being (especially if he believes in death as total loss) to nullify his own self so that the selves of others might remain. Nor would there be any incentive for him to deprive himself of the pleasure of enjoying all he is capable of through injustice so that others may enjoy justice. A rational person does not give something unless he takes a substitute for it. As for giving without a substitute, and leaving without taking—such as dying for the sake of the life of another, or deprivation for the sake of another's enjoyment—the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) rejects it. When they sensed this, the need to repair this deficiency led them to invent these false illusions, which have no place except in the arena of the imagination and the enclosure of fancy. They said: A human being free from the bondage of illusions and superstitions must sacrifice his life for his homeland, or for whatever constitutes his honor, to attain eternal life through good mention and beautiful praise; and he must deprive himself of some of his enjoyments in society so that others may attain them, so that the social order and civilization may be established and social justice completed, thereby attaining a life of honor and loftiness.

I wonder, if there is no human being, and this material composition is nullified, and thereby all its properties—including life and consciousness—are nullified, then who is it that attains this life and this honor? And who is it that perceives it and takes pleasure in it? Is this anything but a superstition?

**Secondly:** The end of the verse—which is His saying, the Exalted: "but you are not aware"—does not suit this meaning. Rather, it would have been appropriate to say: "No, they are living through the survival of their beautiful remembrance and the praise of people for them after them," because that would be suitable for the station of consolation and heart-soothing.

**Thirdly:** The counterpart to this verse—which explains it—describes their life after being killed in a way that contradicts this meaning. The Exalted said: **وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتًا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ** — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord" (3:169), until the end of the verses. It is well known that this life is an external, real life, not a figurative one.

**Fourthly:** Ignorance of this life after death was not entirely far-fetched for some Muslims in the middle of the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). That which is a definitive text (نص) incapable of interpretation (التأويل) is only the Resurrection for the Day of Judgment. As for what is between death and the Gathering—which is the intermediate life (الحيوة البرزخية) — *the state between death and resurrection* — although it is among the true gnoses (المعارف) explained by the Quran, it is not among the necessities (ضروريات) of the Quran. The Muslims are not in consensus (مجمعين) upon it; rather, some deny it even today among those who believe that the soul (النفس) is not immaterial (مجردة) from matter and that the human being's existence is nullified by death and the dissolution of the composition, and then Allah resurrects him for judgment on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, it is possible that what is intended is the explanation of the life of the martyrs in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) due to the ignorance of some believers regarding that, even if others knew it.

In short: What is meant by "life" in the verse is real life, not figurative life. Allah, the Glorified, has counted the life of the disbeliever after his death as destruction and ruin in several places in His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ الْبَوارِ** — "and settled their people in the abode of ruin" (14:28), among other verses. Thus, life is the life of happiness, and those living with this life are the believers specifically, as He said: **وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "and the abode of the Hereafter is indeed the [true] Life, had they known" (29:64). They did not know because their senses are restricted to perceiving the properties of life in worldly matter. As for what is beyond it, if they do not perceive it, they do not distinguish between it and non-existence, so they imagine it to be non-existence. What the imagination (الوهم) conceives is common to both the believer and the disbeliever in this world. Therefore, He said in this verse: "No, they are living, but you are not aware"—meaning, with your senses—just as He said in the other verse: "is indeed the [true] Life, had they known"—meaning, with certainty (اليقين), as the Exalted said: **كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ** — "No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell" (102:5-6).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Do not say of those who are killed in the way of Allah that they are dead, and do not believe in their non-existence and nullification as the word "death" implies to you and its opposition to life, and as your senses suggest to you. They are not dead in the sense of nullification; rather, they are living, but your senses do not attain that nor perceive it. Addressing the believers with this—even though all or most of them knew of the survival of the human life after death and the non-nullification of his essence (الذات)—is only to awaken them and alert them to what is known to them, so that by paying attention to it, the distress may be lifted from their chests and the agitation and anxiety from their hearts when the calamity of being killed strikes them. For with this realization, nothing remains of the effects of being killed for the guardians of the slain except a separation for a few days in this world, which is trivial in comparison to the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified, and what the slain has attained of the good life, the permanent blessing, and "pleasure from Allah which is greater." This is similar to addressing the Prophet with such as His saying, the Exalted: **الْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ** — "The truth is from your Lord; so do not be among the doubters" (2:147), even though he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was the first of those certain of the signs of his Lord. However, it is a speech used as an allusion (كنِّي به) to the clarity of the matter and its manifestation such that it does not accept any mental passing thought to the contrary.

## The Plane of the Intermediate Realm (نشأة البرزخ)

The verse indicates with clear indication the intermediate life (الحيوة البرزخية) of the human being, as does its counterpart verse: **وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتًا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ** — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord" (3:169). The verses regarding this are many.

Among the most strange things is what some people have mentioned regarding the verse: that it was revealed concerning the martyrs of Badr, so it is specific to them only and does not extend to others. Indeed, one of the investigative commentators (المحققين) did well in the commentary on His saying: "And take recourse in patience and prayer," when he asked Allah for patience to endure such statements.

I wonder what these people intend by this statement of theirs? In what manner do they conceive the life of the martyrs of Badr after their being killed, given their claim of the non-existence of the human being after death and killing, and the dissolution and nullification of his composition? Is it by way of a miracle (الإعجاز): by Allah singling them out with a nobility (كرامة) with which He did not honor the Noble Prophet and the rest of the prophets, messengers, and near-stationed saints (الاولياء المقربين), in that Allah specifically maintained their existence after non-existence? This would not be a miracle but the bringing into existence of an impossibility (محال) that is necessarily impossible, and there is no miracle in the impossible. If the intellect allowed the nullification of this ruling despite its self-evidence, no necessary ruling would remain firm, let alone anything below it. Or is it by way of an exception in the ruling of the senses (الحس), such that the senses are mistaken regarding the matter of these martyrs? Thus, they are living, provided for with food, drink, and other enjoyments—while being hidden from the senses—and what the senses attained of their matter through killing, severing of limbs, loss of sensation, and dissolution of composition was entirely mistaken. If such errors were permissible for the senses—being correct in one thing and mistaken in another without a specificator (مخصص)—trust in them would be absolutely nullified. If the specificator were the Divine Will, its attachment would require another specificator. The problem—which is the lack of trust in perception—remains as it is; for it would be possible to find what is not real as real, and the real as not real. How could a rational person be satisfied to utter such a thing? Is it anything but sophistry (سفسطة)?

In this statement of theirs, they have followed the path of the common traditionists (المحدثين), who hold that things hidden from our senses which are indicated by the religious appearances (الظواهر) of the Book and the Sunnah—such as angels, the souls of the believers, and other such things—are material, natural existents and subtle bodies (أجسام لطيفة) that can dwell in and penetrate dense bodies, in the form of a human and the like, performing all human actions, for example, and having powers like ours, except that they are not governed by the laws of nature: such as change, transformation, composition, and its dissolution, and natural life and death. If Allah wills their appearance, they appear to our senses, and if He does not will it, or wills that they do not appear, they do not appear—a pure will without a specificator in the side of the senses or those things.

This statement of theirs is based on the denial of causality (العليَّة والمعلولية) between things. If this false wish were true, all intellectual truths and scientific rulings would be nullified, let alone religious gnoses. The turn would not even reach their subtle, honored bodies which the hand of material, natural influence and being influenced does not reach—and this is obvious.

It has become clear from what has passed: that the verse indicates the intermediate life (الحيوة البرزخيَّة), which is called the world of the grave (عالم القبر), an intermediate world between death and the Resurrection, in which the deceased is blessed or punished until the Resurrection occurs.

Among the verses indicating it—which is a counterpart to this noble verse—is His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتًا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ . فَرِحِينَ بِمَا آتَاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ . يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord, exulting in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and rejoicing for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace, and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful" (3:169-171). The demonstration of the verse's indication of the objective has already passed. If the one who claims these verses are specific to the martyrs of Badr were to contemplate the text of the verses, he would find that their context implies the participation of other believers with them in life and blessing after death.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: **حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ . لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صَالِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ ۚ كَلَّا ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَائِلُهَا ۖ وَمِن وَرَائِهِم بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** — "When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back, that I may act righteously in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says.’ And before them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected" (23:99-100). The verse is clear in its indication that there is an intermediate life between their worldly life and their life after the Resurrection. The full discussion on the verse will come, Allah willing.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا ۗ لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا . يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ** (and it is known that what is meant is the first time they see them, which is the day of death, as other verses indicate) **لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا . وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا . أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا . وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّمَاءُ بِالْغَمَامِ** (and that is the Day of Resurrection) **وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا . الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ ۚ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ عَسِيرًا** — "Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why are the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have been greatly rebellious. The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off!’ We shall set out for the deeds they have done and make them like scattered dust. On that day the inhabitants of paradise will have a better abode and a better resting place. The day when the sky is rent asunder with clouds and the angels are sent down in a grand descent. On that day the true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent, and it will be a hard day for the faithless" (25:21-26). Its indication is clear, and the detailed discussion on it will come in its place, Allah willing.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: **قَالُوا رَبَّنَا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَاعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ** — "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice didst Thou cause us to die, and twice didst Thou give us life, then we confessed our sins. Is there any way out?’" (40:11). Here, until the Day of Resurrection—which is the day of this statement of theirs—there are two causings of death and two givings of life. The meaning will not be sound except by establishing the intermediate realm (البرزخ), such that there is a causing of death and a giving of life in the intermediate realm, and a giving of life on the Day of Resurrection. If one of the two givings of life were in this world and the other in the hereafter, there would be only one causing of death without a second. Discussion related to this station has already passed regarding His saying, the Exalted: **كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا فَأَحْيَاكُمْ** — "How can you deny Allah? You were lifeless and He gave you life" (2:28), so refer back to it.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: **وَحَاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ . النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذَابِ** — "and a terrible punishment besieged Pharaoh’s clan: the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], ‘Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!’" (40:45-46). For it is known that on the Day of Resurrection there is no morning (بكرة) nor evening (عشى); it is a day unlike the [worldly] day.

The verses from which this Quranic reality is benefited, or to which they point, are many, such as His saying, the Exalted: **تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ** — "By Allah, We certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds seem goodly to them. So he is their patron today, and there is a painful punishment for them" (16:63), among others.

## (The Immateriality of the Soul)

Through contemplation (التدبر) on the verse and the other verses we have mentioned, another broader reality becomes clear: the immateriality of the soul (تجرد النفس) — *its being a reality beyond the body, whose ruling is different from the ruling of the body and other physical compositions*. It has a mode of union with the body, managing it through consciousness, will, and other perceptive attributes. Contemplation on the previously mentioned verses clarifies this meaning, for they imply that the human being in his person (بشخصه) is not the body; he does not die with the death of the body, nor does he perish with its perishing, the dissolution of its composition, and the scattering of its parts. He remains after the perishing of the body in a pleasant, eternal life and permanent blessing, or in necessary misery and painful punishment. His happiness in this life and his misery in it are linked to the nature of his traits (ملكاته) and his deeds, not to physical aspects and social rulings.

These are meanings provided by these noble verses, and it is clear that these are rulings that differ from physical rulings and are incompatible with material, worldly properties from all aspects. Thus, the human soul is other than the body.

Among the verses indicating this is His saying, the Exalted: **اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنَامِهَا ۖ فَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضَىٰ عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ الْأُخْرَىٰ** — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep. Then He retains those for whom He has ordained death and releases the others" (39:42). Taking (التوفي) and full retrieval (الإستيفاء) is the taking of a right in its entirety and completeness. What the verse contains of taking, retaining, and releasing is manifest in the distinction between the soul and the body.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ ۚ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَافِرُونَ . قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we indeed be created anew?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord’" (32:10-11). The Glorified mentioned a doubt from the doubts of the disbelievers who deny the Resurrection, which is: after death and the dissolution of the composition of our bodies, our limbs are separated, our parts scattered, and our forms changed, so we are "lost in the earth" and the senses of perceivers lose us; how then is it possible for us to occur a second time in a new creation? This is mere deeming it far-fetched. Allah taught His Messenger the answer to it by His saying: "Say: The angel of death who has been given charge of you will take you fully..." The summary of the answer is that there is an angel in charge of you who takes you fully and receives you; he does not let you be lost, and you are in his grasp and protection. What is lost in the earth is only your bodies, not your souls, which are indicated by the pronoun "you" (كم), for he takes *you* fully.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِهِ** — "and breathed into him of His spirit" (32:9). He mentioned this regarding the creation of the human being, then the Exalted said: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ ۖ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي** — "They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (17:85). Thus, He informed that the spirit (الروح) is of the nature of His command (أمر). Then He defined the command in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ . فَسُبْحَانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83). Thus, He informed that the spirit is from the dominion (الملكوت) and that it is the word "Be" (كن). Then He defined the command by describing it with another description in His saying: **وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ** — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (54:50). The expression "like the twinkling of an eye" indicates that the command, which is the word "Be," is of instantaneous existence (دفعي الوجود), not gradual. It exists without its existence being conditioned or restricted by time or place. From here it becomes clear that the command—and from it the spirit—is something non-physical and non-material. For material, physical existents have as one of their general laws that they are gradual in existence, restricted by time and place. Thus, the spirit that belongs to the human being is not material or physical, even if it has a connection to it.

There are verses that reveal the nature of this connection. The Exalted said: **مِنْهَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ** — "From it We created you" (20:55). And the Exalted said: **خَلَقَ الْإِنسَانَ مِن صَلْصَالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ** — "He created man from dry clay like in pottery" (55:14). And the Exalted said: **وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنسَانِ مِن طِينٍ . ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن مَّاءٍ مَّهِينٍ** — "and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid" (32:7-8). Then He, the Glorified and Exalted, said: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ . ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ . ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَامَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ ۚ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ** — "Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. Then We made him a drop of [seminal] fluid [lodged] in a secure abode. Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh. Then We produced him as yet another creature. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators!" (22:12-14). Thus, He informed that the human being was nothing but a natural body upon which different, changing forms occurred. Then Allah produced this, which was a rigid, lifeless body, as "another creature" possessing consciousness and will, performing actions of consciousness, will, thought, disposal in the realms of existence, and management in the affairs of the world through moving, changing, and transforming—among other things that do not proceed from bodies and physical things. Thus, neither is it physical, nor is its subject that performs it physical.

The soul, in relation to the body whose matter ends in its production—which is the body from which the soul originates—is like the fruit from the tree or the light from the oil, in a remote sense. By this, the nature of its connection to the body at its inception becomes clear; then, through death, the connection is severed and the hold is nullified. In its first existence, it is the very same as the body, then it becomes distinct through its production from it, then it becomes entirely independent of it. This is what the mentioned noble verses provide through their apparent meaning (بظهورها). There are many verses that provide this reality through allusion and pointing, which the insightful contemplator will encounter. And Allah is the Guide.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **We will surely test you with something of fear and hunger, and a loss of wealth and lives and fruits...** (155). When Allah commanded them to seek help through patience (الصبر) — *the steadfast endurance and restraint of the soul* — and prayer (الصلاة), and forbade them from saying that those killed in the way of Allah are dead, but rather that they are alive, He clarified for them the reason for which He addressed them as He did. This reason is that they will be tested by that through which alone high stations are established and affairs are purified in the noble life and the upright religion (الدين الحنيف) — namely, war and fighting. The wheel of victory and triumph does not turn according to their desire unless they fortify themselves with these two strongholds and support themselves with these two powers: patience and prayer. To these, they must add a third quality, which no people have ever maintained without attaining their utmost desire and reaching the pinnacle of their perfection, strengthening their might and comforting their souls. This quality is the faith (الإيمان) — *the firm conviction and heart-felt submission to the truth* — that the slain among them is neither dead nor lost, and that their striving with wealth and soul is neither wasted nor in vain. If they kill their enemy, they are upon life, having annihilated their foe and the oppressive and false rule he desired over them; and if their enemy kills them, they are still upon life, and oppression and falsehood have not gained mastery over them. Thus, they possess one of the two best outcomes in any case.

The general hardships brought about by war are fear, hunger, and the loss of wealth and lives, so Allah the Exalted mentioned them. As for "fruits" (الثمرات), it appears that they refer to children; for the impact of war in reducing progeny through the death of men and youths is more evident than its impact in reducing the fruits of trees. It has also been said that "fruits" refers to the produce of palm trees, namely dates, while "wealth" refers to other things, such as livestock like camels and sheep.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **...and give good news to the patient (155) who, when an affliction visits them, say, ‘Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return’ (156).** He repeated the mention of the patient to first give them glad tidings; second, to clarify the manner of patience by teaching what constitutes "beautiful patience" (الصبر الجميل); third, to manifest the truth of the matter that necessitates the obligation of patience, which is Allah's ownership of man; and fourth, to clarify their general reward—which is blessings (الصلوات), mercy (الرحمة), and guidance (الاهتداء).

Thus, the Exalted first commanded His Prophet to give them glad tidings, without mentioning the object of the tidings, in order to magnify its status; for since it is from Allah, the Glorified, it can only be good and beautiful, and the Lord of Might has guaranteed it. Then He clarified that the patient are those who say such-and-such when an affliction (المصيبة) — *any event that strikes a person* — occurs. The word "affliction" is only used for disliked calamities. It is well-known that the intended "saying" is not merely the verbal utterance of the sentence without the presence of its meaning in the mind, nor is it merely the mental passing of the thought without realizing its reality. Its reality is that man is owned by Allah with a true ownership, and that his return is to Allah, the Glorified. Through this realization, the best form of patience is achieved—that which cuts off the roots of anxiety and regret and washes away the rust of heedlessness (الغفلة).

The explanation of this is that the existence of man and everything that follows his existence—his powers and actions—is essentially sustained by Allah, who is his Originator and Creator. Thus, he is sustained by Him, needy of Him, and dependent upon Him in all his states, both in his coming into being and his persistence, never being independent of Him. Therefore, his Lord has the right to dispose of him as He wills, and man has no say in the matter, as he possesses no independence whatsoever. Thus, Allah possesses true ownership (الملك) over man's existence, powers, and actions.

Furthermore, the Exalted granted him ownership by permission (الإذن) regarding the attribution of his essence; hence it is said: "Man has existence." Likewise, regarding the attribution of his powers and actions; hence it is said: "Man has powers like hearing and sight," and "Man has actions like walking, speaking, eating, and drinking." Were it not for the divine permission, neither man nor any other creature would possess any of these apparent attributions, due to their total lack of independence in existence apart from Allah.

The Glorified has informed us that all things will return to their state prior to this permission, and no ownership will remain except for Allah alone. The Exalted said: **To whom belongs the sovereignty today? To Allah, the One, the All-paramount** (40:16). In this is the return of man, with all that he possesses and all that is with him, to Allah, the Glorified.

Thus, there is a true ownership (ملك حقيقي) which belongs to Allah, the Glorified, in which He has no partner—neither man nor anyone else—and an apparent, formal ownership (ملك ظاهري صوري), such as man's ownership of himself, his children, his wealth, and so on. The latter belongs to Allah, the Glorified, in reality, and to man through the Exalted's granting of ownership, metaphorically in appearance. When man remembers the reality of Allah's ownership and relates it to himself, he finds himself to be the absolute property of his Lord. When he also remembers that the apparent ownership between humans—including his ownership of himself, his wealth, and his children—will be nullified and return to his Lord, he realizes that in the end, he owns nothing at all, neither in reality nor metaphorically. When this is the case, there is no meaning for being affected by the afflictions that cause distress when they strike. For distress only arises from a person losing something he owns, such that he rejoices at its presence and grieves at its loss. But if he submits and believes that he owns nothing, he will not be distressed or grieve. How can one be distressed who believes that Allah alone possesses the kingdom, disposing of His property as He wills?

## ( Ethics )

Know that the refinement of the soul's ethics (الأخلاق) and its dispositions (الملكات) in both the cognitive and practical dimensions—the acquisition of virtuous traits and the removal of vice—is only achieved through the repetition of righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) appropriate to them, practicing them, and persisting in them. This continues until particular knowledges (علوم جزئية) from particular instances become established in the soul, accumulating and becoming engraved in the soul in a manner that is impossible or difficult to erase. For example, if a person wishes to remove the trait of cowardice (الجبن) and acquire the disposition of courage (الشجاعة), he must repeatedly enter into hardships and terrors that make hearts tremble and innards quake. Every time he enters such a situation and witnesses that he was capable of entering it, and perceives the sweetness of boldness and the hideousness of flight and caution, his soul is engraved with this, layer upon layer, until the disposition of courage is established within it. Although the attainment of this cognitive disposition is not itself by choice, it is voluntary and acquired (كسبي) through the preliminary steps leading to it, as you have seen.

Once you understand what we have mentioned, you will know that the path to refining ethics and acquiring virtues follows one of two methods:

**The First Method:** Refining them through righteous worldly goals and knowledges and opinions that are praised among people. For instance, it is said: "Chastity (العفة), contentment with what one has, and refraining from what others possess bring about honor and greatness in the eyes of people and status among the masses; greed (الشره) causes destitution and poverty; avarice (الطمع) causes the humiliation of the noble soul; knowledge (العلم) brings the attention of the masses, honor, prestige, and intimacy with the elite; knowledge is a sight by which man avoids every disliked thing and attains every beloved thing, while ignorance (الجهل) is blindness; knowledge guards you while you guard wealth; courage (الشجاعة) is a stability that prevents the soul from vacillation and brings praise from people in any case, whether one conquers or is conquered, unlike cowardice or recklessness; and justice (العدالة) is the soul's rest from harmful anxieties, and it is life after death through the persistence of one's name, good remembrance, beautiful praise, and love in the hearts."

This is the well-known method upon which the science of ethics (علم الأخلاق) was structured, and it is what has been transmitted from the inquiries of the ancients among the Greeks and others.

The Quran did not employ this method, which is based on choosing what is praised by the general public over what is blamed by them, and adopting what society deems good while abandoning what it deems ugly. Yes, the speech of the Exalted may occasionally follow this course in matters that actually relate to an otherworldly reward or punishment, such as His saying: **...and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it, so that the people may have no argument against you...** (2:150). The Glorified called for resolve and steadfastness, justifying it by saying: "so that there may not be..." Similarly, His saying: **...and do not quarrel, lest you lose heart and your power depart; and be patient...** (8:46). The Glorified called for patience and justified it by stating that abandoning it and creating discord causes loss of heart, the departure of power, and the emboldening of the enemy. And His saying: **...but whoever is patient and forgives—that is indeed the course of resolve** (42:43). He called for patience and forgiveness, justifying it by resolve and greatness.

**The Second Method:** Otherworldly goals. These are frequently mentioned in the speech of the Exalted, such as His saying: **Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise to be theirs...** (9:111), and His saying: **...Indeed the patient will be paid their reward without any reckoning** (39:10), and His saying: **...Indeed for the wrongdoers there is a painful punishment** (14:22), and His saying: **Allah is the wali of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light. As for the faithless, their awliya are the fake deities: they bring them out of light into darkness...** (2:257). There are many such examples across various styles.

To this category belongs another type of verse, such as His saying: **No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah** (57:22). This verse calls for the abandonment of regret and exultation on the basis that what struck you was not meant to miss you, and what missed you was not meant to strike you, because events are attributed to a decreed judgment (قضاء مقضى) and a measured destiny (قدر مقدر). Thus, regret and exultation are futile acts that should not proceed from a believer who believes in Allah, in whose hand are the reins of all affairs, as indicated by His saying: **No affliction visits except by Allah’s leave. Whoever believes in Allah, He guides his heart...** (64:11). This category of verses is also similar to the previous one, which seeks to refine ethics through noble otherworldly goals—which are real perfections, not merely conjectural ones—by utilizing the preceding real principles of destiny (القدر), judgment (القضاء), adopting the character of Allah, and remembering the Beautiful Names of Allah and His Sublime Attributes, and the like.

**If you say:** Seeking cause through things like judgment and destiny necessitates the nullification of the laws of this voluntary realm (النشأة الاختيارية), and in that is the nullification of virtuous ethics and the disruption of the order of this natural realm. For if it were permissible to rely—in refining the trait of patience and steadfastness and abandoning exultation and regret, as derived from the previous verse—on the fact that events are written in a Preserved Tablet and decreed by an inevitable judgment, it would be possible to rely on that to abandon seeking sustenance, acquiring every desired perfection, and avoiding every ethical vice and otherwise. It would then be permissible to sit back from seeking sustenance, defending the truth, and the like, by saying that what will occur is already decreed and written. Likewise, it would be permissible to abandon striving to acquire every perfection and leave every deficiency by relying on the inevitability of judgment and the reality of the Book; and in that is the nullification of all perfection.

**I say:** We have already mentioned in the discussion on judgment (القضاء) that which clarifies the answer to this problem. We mentioned there that human actions are among the parts of the causes (علل) of events. It is well-known that the existence of effects (المعاليل) and results (المسببات) depends on the existence of their causes and the parts of their causes. Thus, the statement of one who says: "Satiety is either decreed to exist or decreed to be absent, and in either case, eating has no effect," is a gross error. For the assumption of the realization of satiety in the external world is only sound after assuming the realization of voluntary eating, which is one of the parts of its causes. It is an error for a person to assume an effect among effects and then judge the cancellation of its causes or any part of the parts of its causes.

Therefore, it is not permissible for a person to nullify the law of choice (الاختيار) upon which the cycle of his worldly life turns, and to which his happiness and wretchedness are attributed. It is one of the parts of the causes of the events that attach to his existence from his actions, or the states and dispositions resulting from his actions. However, just as it is not permissible for him to exclude his will and choice from the group of causes and nullify its law of influence, it is likewise not permissible for him to judge that his choice is the sole cause and the complete cause (علة تامة) to which events are attributed, without anything else from the parts of the world and the causes existing within it—at the head of which is the Divine Will—participating with it. For from this [erroneous view] branch out many blameworthy traits such as vanity (العجب), pride (الكبر), stinginess (البخل), exultation, regret, grief, and the like.

The ignorant person says: "I am the one who did such-and-such and abandoned such-and-such," so he becomes vain with himself, or acts proudly toward others, or is stingy with his wealth—being ignorant that the rest of the causes outside his deficient choice, which are thousands upon thousands, had they not prepared the matter for him, his choice would have availed nothing and been of no use. The ignorant person says: "If only I had done such-and-such, I would not have been harmed by such-and-such, or such-and-such would not have escaped me," being ignorant that this loss or death—meaning the absence of profit, health, or life—is attributed to thousands upon thousands of causes, the absence of any one of which is sufficient for their absence—meaning the realization of the loss or death—even if his choice were present. Furthermore, the very choice of man is attributed to many causes outside of man's choice; for choice itself is not by choice.

So if you recognize what we have mentioned—which is a Quranic reality provided by divine teaching as previously stated—and then you contemplate the noble verses in this regard, you will find that the Quran relies on the inevitable judgment and the Preserved Tablet in refining some ethics but not others.

As for those actions, states, or dispositions whose attribution to judgment and destiny would necessitate the nullification of the law of choice, the Quran does not rely on it there; rather, it rejects it completely, as in His saying: **When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecencies. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?’** (7:28).

But as for those things where the denial of their attribution to judgment would necessitate the affirmation of the independence of man's choice in influence, and his being a complete cause not needing anything else for influence and being independent of others, the Quran affirms their attribution to judgment. It guides man to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) which does not fail its traveler, until the vicious traits that follow it are eliminated—such as attributing events to judgment so that man does not exult in what he has gained out of ignorance, nor grieve over what he has lost out of ignorance, as in His saying: **...and give them out of the wealth of Allah which He has given you** (24:33). This calls for generosity by attributing wealth to Allah's giving. And as in His saying: **...and spend out of what We have provided them** (2:3). This encourages spending by relying on the fact that it is from Allah's provision. And as in His saying: **You might perhaps fret yourself to death with grief on their account if they do not believe in this discourse. Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct** (18:6-7). He forbade His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) from grief and sorrow by relying on the fact that their disbelief is not a victory for them over Allah, the Glorified; rather, everything on earth are matters placed upon it for trial and examination, and so on.

This method—meaning the second way of refining ethics—is the way of the prophets, and there is much of it in the Quran and in what has been transmitted to us from the heavenly books.

**And here is a third method** exclusive to the Holy Quran, not found in anything transmitted to us from the heavenly books, the teachings of past prophets (سلام الله عليهم أجمعين), or the transmitted knowledge of the divine sages. It is the cultivation of man, both in disposition and knowledge, by employing knowledges and insights (معارف) through which the very subject of the vices no longer remains. In other words, it is the removal of vicious traits by "lifting" (الرفع) — *removing the very basis of the trait* — rather than by "repelling" (الدفع) — *struggling against its manifestation*.

This is because for every action intended for other than Allah, the Glorified, the desired goal is either an honor (عزة) in the object which is craved, or a power (قوة) which is feared and guarded against. But Allah, the Glorified, says: **...Indeed all might belongs to Allah...** (10:65), and He says: **...that power belongs entirely to Allah...** (2:165). Realization of this true knowledge leaves no subject for ostentation (الرياء), seeking reputation (السمعة), fear of other than Allah, hope in other than Him, or reliance upon other than Him. These two propositions, when they become known to man, wash away every vice, whether in disposition or action, and adorn his soul with the corresponding noble divine traits, such as godwariness (التقوى) through Allah, seeking honor through Allah, and others like dignity, majesty, independence, and divine, lordly awe.

Also, it is repeated in His speech, the Exalted, that the kingdom belongs to Allah, and that to Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, as has been explained repeatedly. The reality of this ownership, as is apparent, leaves no independence for any of the existents apart from Him, nor any self-sufficiency from Him in any way. There is nothing except that He, the Glorified, is the Owner of its essence and everything belonging to its essence. Man's faith in this ownership and his realization of it necessitates the fall of all things—in essence, attribute, and action—from the degree of independence in his eyes. Such a person cannot desire other than His Face, the Exalted, nor can he humble himself to anything, or fear or hope in anything, or take pleasure or rejoice in anything, or rely on anything, or trust in anything, or submit to anything, or delegate to anything, other than His Face, the Exalted. In short, he does not desire or seek anything except His True Face, which remains after the annihilation of everything else. He does not turn away or flee except from falsehood—which is other than Him—whose existence he sees as having no weight and which he does not value in the face of the Truth, which is the existence of his Creator, may His majesty be exalted.

Likewise is His saying: **Allah—there is no god except Him. To Him belong the Best Names** (20:8), and His saying: **That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things...** (6:102), and His saying: **...who perfected everything He created...** (32:7), and His saying: **All faces shall be humbled before the Living One, the All-sustainer...** (20:111), and His saying: **...all are obedient to Him** (2:116), and His saying: **Your Lord has decided that you shall worship none but Him...** (17:23), and His saying: **...Is it not sufficient regarding your Lord that He is witness to all things?** (41:53), and His saying: **...Look! He indeed encompasses all things** (41:54), and His saying: **and that the terminus is toward your Lord** (53:42).

From this category are the verses we are currently discussing, namely His saying: **...and give good news to the patient who, when an affliction visits them, say, ‘Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return’** to the end. These verses and their like contain specific divine insights with specific real results. Their cultivation does not resemble the type of cultivation intended by an ethical philosopher in his field, nor the type of cultivation established by the prophets in their laws. For the first method, as you have seen, is based on general social beliefs regarding beauty and ugliness; the second method is based on general religious beliefs regarding the duties of servitude and their rewards; but this third method is based on pure and perfect monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — which is unique to Islam, upon its Lawgiver and his Progeny be the best of blessings.

If you are to wonder, then wonder at the statement of some Western orientalist scholars in their history discussing Islamic civilization, the gist of which is: "What the researcher must pay attention to is the study of the civilizational affairs that the Islamic religious call spread among its followers, and the advantages and characteristics it left and bequeathed to them in terms of the advancement of culture and the elevation of civilization. As for the religious knowledges contained in Islam, they are ethical materials shared by all prophethoods and called for by all prophets." This is the end of his statement.

By encompassing what we have presented of the explanation, you will recognize the failure of his view and the confusion of his opinion. For the result is a branch of its premise, and the external effects resulting from cultivation are but the offspring and results of the type of sciences and knowledges received by the student being cultivated. A word that calls to a lower truth and a moderate perfection is not equal to a word that calls to the pure Truth and the ultimate perfection. This is the state of this third method: the first method calls to social truth; the second calls to the actual truth and real perfection in which lies man's happiness in his afterlife; and the third calls to the Truth which is Allah, and builds its cultivation on the fact that Allah, the Glorified, is One without partner, resulting in pure servitude (العبودية). How great is the difference between these methods!

This method has gifted human society a vast multitude of righteous servants, lordly scholars, and near-stationed saints (الأولياء المقربين), both men and women; and that is sufficient honor for the religion.

Furthermore, this method may differ from the other two methods in terms of results, for its foundation is upon servile love (الحب العبودي) and preferring the side of the Lord over the side of the servant. It is well-known that love, infatuation, and devotion may lead the lover to matters that the social intellect—which is the criterion for social ethics—or the ordinary general understanding—which is the basis for general religious duties—might not approve. The intellect has its laws, and love has its laws. The clarification of this meaning will come in some of the upcoming discussions, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **It is they who receive the blessings from their Lord and [His] mercy, and it is they who are the [rightly] guided** (157). Contemplation of the verse suggests that blessings (الصلوة) are distinct from mercy (الرحمة) in one respect. This is evidenced by the pluralization of "blessings" and the singularization of "mercy." The Exalted has said: **It is He who blesses you, and His angels [as well], that He may bring you out from darkness into light, and He is most merciful to the faithful** (33:43). The verse implies that His saying, "and He is most merciful to the faithful," is in the position of the cause for His saying, "It is He who blesses you." The meaning is that He only blesses you—and that was a necessary and expected matter—because His habit has been mercy toward the believers, and you are believers; so it was your right that He blesses you so that He may have mercy on you. Thus, the relation of the blessing to the mercy is like the relation of the preliminary to its objective, and like the relation between a glance and a gaze, or between casting into fire and burning, for example. This fits what has been said regarding the meaning of *salat* (الصلوة): that it is an inclination and a leaning. Thus, the blessing from Allah, the Glorified, is an inclination toward the servant with mercy; from the angels, it is an inclination toward man by mediating the delivery of mercy; and from the believers, it is a return and a prayer through servitude. This does not contradict the fact that the blessing itself is a mercy and one of its instances. For mercy in the Quran, as suggested by contemplation of its occurrences, is the absolute divine gift and the general lordly bestowal, as the Exalted said: **...and My mercy embraces all things...** (7:156), and the Exalted said: **Your Lord is the All-sufficient, the Lord of mercy. If He wishes, He will take you away, and make whomever He wishes succeed you, just as He produced you from the descendants of another people** (6:133). Thus, the taking away is due to His self-sufficiency, while the succession and production are due to His mercy; and both are attributed to His mercy just as they are attributed to His self-sufficiency. Every creation and command is a mercy, just as every creation and command is a gift that requires self-sufficiency. The Exalted said: **...and the giving of your Lord is not confined** (17:20). If His gift is the blessing, then it too is from mercy, except that it is a specific mercy. From here, the pluralization of "blessings" and the singularization of "mercy" in the verse can be justified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **...and it is they who are the [rightly] guided.** It is as if it were in the position of the result of His saying: "It is they who receive the blessings from their Lord and [His] mercy." For this reason, He renewed their guidance in a second sentence separate from the first, and did not say: "blessings from their Lord, mercy, and guidance," nor did He say: "and it is they who are the guided ones (المهديون)." Rather, He mentioned their acceptance of guidance by using the term "the [rightly] guided" (المهتدون), which is a branch resulting from the act of guiding. Thus, it has become clear that the mercy is His guiding them to Himself, the blessings are like the preliminaries to this guidance, and their being "rightly guided" is the result of this guidance. So each of the blessings, mercy, and being rightly guided is distinct from the other, even if all are mercy from another perspective.

The example of these believers in what Allah informs of His honor toward them is like a friend of yours whom you meet while he is heading toward your house, asking about it and intending to stay with you. You meet him with joy and honor, and you lead him on the straight path while you are with him, walking him along and not letting him go astray in his journey until you bring him to his lodging in your house. You attend to him on the way with his food, drink, mount, and travel, and protect him from every disliked thing that might strike him. All these matters are a single honoring, because you only intend his honor. Yet every act of attention is a specific attention and honor, and the guidance is distinct from the honoring and the attention, while still being an honor. Each of them is an act of attention, each is a guidance, and each is a specific honor, and the whole is an honor. The single general honor is in the position of mercy, the attentions at every moment are in the position of the blessings, and the arrival at the house is in the position of being rightly guided.

The use of the nominal sentence in His saying: **...and it is they who are the [rightly] guided**, the beginning with the demonstrative pronoun indicating distance (أولئك), the second use of the pronoun of separation (هم), and the defining of the predicate with the relative article in "the [rightly] guided" (المهتدون)—all of this is to magnify and glorify their status—and Allah knows best.

## (Narrative Discussion)

### On the Intermediate Realm (البرزخ) and the Life of the Soul After Death

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, narrated from Suwayd ibn Ghaflah from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: "When the son of Adam is in his last day of the world and his first day of the Hereafter, his wealth, his children, and his deeds are personified before him. He turns to his wealth and says: 'By Allah, I was greedy and stingy over you; what do you have for me?' It replies: 'Take from me your shroud.' Then he turns to his children and says: 'By Allah, I was loving toward you and protective of you; what do you have for me?' They reply: 'We shall convey you to your pit and conceal you within it.' Then he turns to his deeds and says: 'By Allah, I was indifferent toward you, and you were heavy upon me; what do you have for me?' It replies: 'I am your companion in your grave and on the day of your gathering, until you and I are presented before your Lord.'

If he was a friend of Allah, there comes to him the person with the most pleasant fragrance, the most beautiful appearance, and the finest raiment, saying: 'Rejoice in rest (روح) from Allah, fragrant herbs (ريحان), and a garden of bliss; you have arrived with the best of arrivals.' He asks: 'Who are you?' It replies: 'I am your righteous deeds (عملك الصالح); depart from the world to Paradise.' Indeed, he recognizes the one who washes him and implores the one who carries him to hasten. When he enters his grave, two angels come to him—the two testers of the grave—dragging their hair and furrowing the earth with their tusks. Their voices are like crashing thunder and their eyes like flashing lightning. They say to him: 'Who is your Lord? Who is your Prophet? And what is your religion?' He replies: 'Allah is my Lord, Muhammad is my prophet, and Islam is my religion.' They say: 'May Allah keep you firm in that which He loves and is pleased with.' This is the meaning of the saying of Allah: **يُثَبِّتُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world' [14:27]. Then they expand his grave for him as far as his eye can see, open for him a door to Paradise, and say: 'Sleep with a cool eye, the sleep of a pampered youth.' This is His saying: **أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا** — 'The inhabitants of paradise on that day will be in a better abode and a better resting place' [25:24].

But if he was an enemy to his Lord, there comes to him the foulest of Allah’s creation in raiment and the most putrid in smell, saying: 'Rejoice in an entertainment of boiling water and the burning of hellfire.' Indeed, he recognizes the one who washes him and implores the one who carries him to hold him back. When he is entered into his grave, the two examiners of the grave come to him, strip his shrouds from him, and say: 'Who is your Lord? Who is your Prophet? And what is your religion?' He says: 'I do not know.' They say to him: 'You did not know, nor were you guided.' Then they strike him with a mallet, a blow such that every creature Allah has created—save for the two heavy weights (the jinn and humans)—is terrified by it. Then they open for him a door to the Fire and say: 'Sleep in the worst condition.' He is then subjected to such constriction that it is like a spearhead being forced into its socket, until his brain exits from between his nails and flesh. Allah sets upon him the snakes, scorpions, and vermin of the earth to bite him until Allah resurrects him from his grave. Indeed, he wishes for the onset of the Hour because of the evil state he is in."

In *Muntakhab al-Basa'ir*, from Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "None are questioned in the grave except those who were purely faithful (محض الإيمان) or purely faithless (محض الكفر)." I said to him: "What about the rest of the people?" He replied: "They are overlooked."

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, from Ibn Zabyan, who said: "I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) and he said: 'What do the people say regarding the souls of the believers after their death?' I said: 'They say they are in the crops of green birds.' He said: 'Subhan Allah! The believer is more honorable to Allah than that! When that [death] occurs, the Messenger of Allah, Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn (peace be upon them) come to him, accompanied by the archangels of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.'"

"If Allah enables his tongue to speak the testimony of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — for Him, and prophethood for the Prophet, and the guardianship (الولاية) — *the divinely appointed authority and leadership* — for the People of the House (أهل البيت), then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, al-Husayn (peace be upon them), and the archangels with them bear witness to that. If his tongue is tied, Allah grants His Prophet the knowledge of what is in his heart regarding that, so he bears witness to it, and Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn—upon all of them be the best of Allah's peace—and the angels present with them bear witness to the Prophet's testimony. When Allah takes him to Himself, He transforms that soul into the Paradise in a form like his [earthly] form; they eat and drink, and when a newcomer arrives, they recognize him by that form which he had in the world."

In *al-Mahasin*, from Hammad ibn Uthman from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "He mentioned the souls—the souls of the believers—and said: 'They meet one another.' I asked: 'They meet?' He said: 'Yes, they question one another and recognize one another, such that if you saw him, you would say: So-and-so.'"

In *al-Kafi*, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The believer visits his family and sees what he loves while what he dislikes is hidden from him. The disbeliever visits his family and sees what he dislikes while what he loves is hidden from him." He said: "Among them are those who visit every Friday, and among them are those who visit according to their deeds."

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The souls are in the likeness of bodies in the trees of Paradise, recognizing and questioning one another. When a soul arrives among the [other] souls, they say: 'Leave it, for it has just approached from a great terror.' Then they ask it: 'What did so-and-so do? And what did so-and-so do?' If it says to them: 'I left him alive,' they hold hope for him. If it says to them: 'He has perished,' they say: 'He has fallen, he has fallen (into the abyss).'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — are numerous. We have only cited those that contain the comprehensive meanings of the *Barzakh*. Regarding the meanings conveyed, there are many widely transmitted (مستفيضة) narrations, and they contain evidence for an existence (نشأة) that is immaterial (مجردة عن المادة).

## ( Philosophical Discussion )

Is the soul (النفس) immaterial (مجردة) — *free from the constraints of matter*? (By "soul," we mean that which each of us refers to by saying "I"; and by its "immateriality," we mean its not being a material thing (أمراً مادياً) characterized by divisibility (انقسام), time (زمان), and space (مكان)).

We do not doubt that we find within ourselves the observation of a meaning we express as "I." Nor do we doubt that every human being is like us in this observation, from which we are never heedless at any moment of our life and consciousness. It is not any of our organs or the parts of our body that we perceive through the senses or through some form of inference—such as our external organs perceived by the external senses like sight and touch, or our internal organs which we have come to know through sensation and experiment. For we may be heedless of every one of them, or any group of them, even the complete collection we call the "body" (البدن); yet we are never heedless of that observed reality which we express as "I." Therefore, it is other than the body and its parts.

Furthermore, if it were the body, or one of its organs or parts, or a property (خاصة) among the properties existing within them—all of which are material (مادية), and the law of matter is gradual change (التغير التدريجي) and the susceptibility to division (الانقسام) and fragmentation (التجزي)—then it would be material, changing, and divisible. But this is not the case. For every individual, when returning to this psychological observation (المشاهدة النفسانية) inherent to themselves, and recalling what they found of this observation since their first consciousness of self, finds it to be a single, observed meaning (معنى مشهوداً واحداً) that remains as it is without the slightest multiplicity or change. This is while they find their body, its parts, and the properties existing with them to be changing and transforming in every respect—in their matter, shape, and all other states and forms. Similarly, one finds it to be a simple (بسيطاً) — *non-composite* — meaning, incapable of division or fragmentation, whereas the body, its parts, and its properties—and indeed every matter and material thing—are as such. Thus, the soul is not the body, nor a part of its parts, nor a property of its properties, whether we perceive it through the senses, through inference, or not at all. For all of these are material however they are conceived, and the law of matter is change and divisibility, while the premise is that our observed self, called the soul, possesses none of these laws. Therefore, the soul is not material in any way.

Moreover, that which we observe, we observe as a single, simple thing in which there is no multiplicity of parts nor any external mixture; rather, it is pure oneness (واحد صرف). Every human being observes this of themselves and sees that they are themselves and not another. Thus, this observed reality is an independent entity (أمرٌ مستقل) in itself; the definition of matter does not apply to it, nor are any of its necessary laws found within it. It is, therefore, an immaterial substance (جوهرٌ مجرد) — *an essential reality existing independently of matter* — attached to the body in a manner that necessitates a certain union with it, which is the attachment of governance (التعلق التدبيري). This is the desired conclusion.

The immateriality of the soul has been denied by all materialists (الماديين), as well as a group of theists among the theologians (المتكلمين) and the literalists (الظاهريين) among the traditionists (المحدثين). They have argued for this denial and attempted to refute the aforementioned proof with arguments that are not free from futile affectation.

The materialists say: Scientific research, despite its advancement and its reaching the utmost precision in investigation today, has not found any bodily property except that it found its material cause. Nor has it found any spiritual effect that does not conform to the laws of matter such that one would judge, because of it, the existence of an immaterial soul.

They say: The nervous system conveys perceptions to the central organ, which is the cerebral part, successively and with extreme speed. Within it, there is a unified collection with a single position whose parts are not distinguished, nor is the cessation of some and the replacement by others perceived. This resultant unity is our "self" which we observe and refer to as "I." Thus, our perception that it is other than all our organs is correct, but it does not prove that it is other than the body and its properties. Rather, it is a unified collection due to the succession and influx [of perceptions] from which we are not heedless. For heedlessness of it—as has been clarified—necessitates the failure of the nerves and the cessation of their functions, which is death. Our perception that it is stable is correct, but not because of its inherent stability or lack of change; rather, the matter is confused for the observer due to the succession of perceptual inputs and the speed of their arrival. It is like a basin into which water enters from one side and exits from the other in equal measure, remaining always full; the senses find the water within it to be one and stable, while in reality, it is neither one nor stable. Similarly, one finds the reflection of a person or a tree in it to be one and stable, yet it is not one and stable, but rather many and gradually changing due to the gradual flow of the water's parts. In this manner exists the stability, unity, and personality (الشخصية) that we see in the soul.

They say: The soul, whose immateriality is argued for through internal observation, is in reality a collection of natural properties—namely, the neural perceptions which are the results of the mutual action and reaction between a part of external matter and a part of the neural complex. Its unity is a social unity (وحدة اجتماعية), not a real, ontological unity (وحدة واقعية حقيقية).

**I say:** As for their saying that scientific research based on sense and experiment has not encountered the soul in its precise journey, nor found a law that is inexplicable except by it—this is a true statement without doubt. However, it does not result in the negation of the immaterial soul whose existence has been proven by demonstration. For the natural sciences, which investigate the laws of nature and the properties of matter, are only capable of attaining the properties of their subject—which is matter—and proving what is of its kind. Likewise, the material tools and properties we use to complete material experiments can only judge material matters. As for what lies beyond matter and nature, these sciences have no right to judge it, neither by negation nor by affirmation. The most that material research can suggest is "non-finding" (عدم الوجدان), and "non-finding" is not "non-existence" (عدم الوجود). It is not within its capacity, as you have known, to find an immaterial thing—outside the kind of matter and the law of nature—among the matter that is its subject, or among the laws and properties of matter that are the results of its research.
