What emboldened them toward this negation was their assumption that those who affirm this immaterial soul only did so because they encountered vital laws among the functions of organs and were unable to provide a scientific explanation for them, so they affirmed an immaterial soul to be the subject and principle of these actions. Thus, when science today attained their natural causes, no reason remained for affirming it. This assumption is similar to what they assumed regarding the proof of the Creator.

This is a corrupt misunderstanding. For those who affirm the existence of this soul did not affirm it for that reason, nor did they attribute some bodily actions to the body where their causes were apparent, and others to the soul where their causes were unknown. Rather, they attributed all of them to bodily causes without intermediary, and to the soul through their mediation. They only attributed to the soul that which cannot possibly be attributed to the body at all—namely, the human being's knowledge of themselves and their observation of their own essence, as mentioned previously.

As for their saying that the "I-ness" (الإنية) observed by the human being as characterized by unity is merely a number of neural perceptions arriving at the center successively and with extreme speed—possessing a social unity—this is a meaningless statement that does not correspond to psychological observation at all. It is as if they became oblivious to their own psychological observation and turned away from it toward the arrival of sensory observations to the brain, occupying themselves with the subsequent effects necessitated by that. I wonder: if it is assumed that there are many things in reality with no unity at all, and these many things—which are perceptions—are material things behind which there is nothing else but themselves, and that the observed thing which is the single soul is the very same as these many perceptions, then from where did this "one" arise, other than which we observe nothing? And from where did this unity, witnessed clearly within it, arise? What they mentioned regarding its "social unity" is a statement more akin to a joke than a serious argument. For a social unity is many in reality without unity; its unity is only in the senses or the imagination—like a single house or a single line, for example—not in itself. In the context of our discussion, the premise is that the many perceptions and consciousnesses are, in themselves, a single consciousness to themselves. Thus, the necessary conclusion of their statement is that these perceptions are many in themselves, not returning to any unity at all, yet they are identically a single psychological consciousness in reality. There is no other entity that possesses these many perceptions and thus perceives them as a unity, as the senses or imagination perceive many sensed or imagined things gathered under the description of social unity. For the premise is that the collection of many perceptions is itself the single psychological perception. If it were said that the perceiver here is the cerebral part, perceiving the many perceptions as a unity, the problem would remain as it is. For the premise is that the perception of the cerebral part is these very many successive perceptions themselves, not that the cerebral part has a power of perception that attaches to these perceptions as the sensory powers attach to their external objects and abstract sensory forms from them. Understand this well.

The discussion regarding how stability (الثبات) and simplicity (البساطة) obtain in this observed reality—which is changing and divisible in itself—is the same as the discussion regarding how its unity obtains.

Furthermore, this assumption—namely, that the many successive perceptions are sensed by a cerebral consciousness as a unity—is itself an incorrect assumption. For what is the status of the brain, the power within it, the consciousness it possesses, and the known object present with it? They are all material things. The characteristic of matter and the material is multiplicity, change, and susceptibility to division. Yet there is nothing of these descriptions and traits in this cognitive form (الصورة العلمية), and there is nothing there other than matter and the material.

Their saying that the matter is confused for the senses or the perceiving power, such that it perceives the many, divisible, and changing as one, simple, and stable, is a clear error. For error and confusion are relative matters (الامور النسبية) that occur through comparison and relation, not psychological matters (الامور النفسية) in themselves. An example of this is that we observe the great celestial bodies as small, like white dots, and we err in this observation according to what scientific proofs demonstrate—as is the case with many of our sensory observations. However, these errors only occur and exist when we compare what is present with the senses to what is in the external reality of these observed things. As for what is present with the senses in itself, it is a factual reality (أمرٌ واقعي)—like a white dot—and there is no meaning to it being an error at all.

The matter in our discussion is of this sort. For if our senses and perceiving powers find many, changing, and divisible things as characterized by unity, stability, and simplicity, the perceiving powers would be erroneous in their perception and confused in their known object in comparison to the known object in the external world. But as for this cognitive form existing with the power, it is absolutely one, stable, and simple in itself. It cannot be said of something with such a status that it is material, due to its lacking the general characteristics of matter.

Thus, it is concluded from all we have mentioned that the argument brought by the materialists through the path of sense and experiment only results in "non-finding." They have fallen into a fallacy by taking "non-existence" (which is their claim) in place of "non-finding." What they depicted to account for the psychological observation—which proves the existence of a single, simple, stable entity—is a corrupt depiction that accords neither with the material principles established by sense and experiment, nor with the reality of the matter as it is in itself.

As for what researchers in modern psychology have hypothesized regarding the soul—that it is the unified state resulting from the interaction of spiritual states such as perception, will, satisfaction, love, and others, producing a unified, composite state—we have no dispute with it. For every researcher has the right to hypothesize a subject and place it as the subject of his research. The discussion is only regarding its existence or non-existence in the external world and reality, regardless of the hypothesis of the hypothesizer. This is the philosophical inquiry, as is apparent to one expert in the aspects of research.

Another group among the deniers of the soul's immateriality from the people of religion said: What is concluded from matters related to human life, such as anatomy and physiology, is that these spiritual vital properties are based on the germs of life and the cells (السلوّلات) which are the foundations of life in humans and other animals, and are attached to them. Thus, the spirit is a property and a specific effect within them; for each individual, there are multiple spirits. That which a human calls the spirit of his self and refers to as "I" is a collection formed from innumerable spirits in a manner of union and gathering. It is known that these vital qualities and spiritual properties cease with the death of the germs and cells and are corrupted by their corruption. Thus, there is no meaning to a single, immaterial soul that remains after the annihilation of the bodily composition. The most that can be said is that since the material principles discovered by scientific research did not suffice to uncover the secrets of life, we may say that natural causes do not suffice to produce the spirit, so it is the effect of another being beyond nature. As for arguing for the immateriality of the soul from the perspective of pure reason, it is something that modern sciences neither accept nor heed, due to their lack of reliance on anything other than sense and experiment. This is their position.

**I say:** You are well aware that everything we brought against the argument of the materialists applies to this fabricated argument without difference. We add that it is flawed **firstly:** because the insufficiency of the scientific principles discovered to this day in explaining the reality of the spirit and life does not result in their eternal insufficiency, nor in the non-termination of these properties to material causes in the reality of the matter despite our ignorance. Is this anything but a fallacy in which "knowledge of non-existence" is placed in the stead of "non-existence of knowledge"?

**Secondly:** because attributing some events of the world—namely, material events—to matter, and others—namely, vital events—to an entity beyond matter (the Creator), is a claim of two principles in creation. Neither the materialist nor the theist accepts this, and all the proofs of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — refute it.

There are other objections they have raised against the immateriality of the soul mentioned in philosophical and theological books. However, all of them arise from a lack of reflection and deep consideration of the aforementioned proof, and a lack of steadfastness in conceptualizing its purpose. Therefore, we have refrained from citing them and discussing them. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to their proper places. And Allah is the Guide.

## ( Ethical Discussion )

The science of ethics (علم الأخلاق) — *the art that investigates human traits (الملكات) related to one's vegetative, animal, and human powers, and distinguishes virtues from vices so that the human being may complete their cognitive happiness by adorning themselves with them, resulting in actions that bring general praise and beautiful commendation from human society* — discovers through its investigation that human ethics terminate in three general powers within him. These are what prompt the soul to adopt the practical sciences upon which the actions of the species are based and terminated, and their preparation and mobilization within him. These are the three powers: the appetitive (الشهوية), the irascible (الغضبية), and the rational-reflective (النطقية الفكرية).

All works and actions proceeding from the human being are either of the kind of actions attributed to the seeking of benefit—such as eating, drinking, dressing, and others; or of the actions attributed to the repelling of harm—such as the human being's defense of themselves, their honor, their wealth, and the like. These actions proceed from the irascible principle, just as the previous category proceeds from the appetitive principle. Or they are from the works attributed to conceptualization and reflective assent—such as the composition of syllogisms and the establishment of proof and others. These actions proceed from the rational-reflective power.

Since the essence of the human being is as if composed of these three powers—by whose union and the resulting composite unity specific species-actions proceed, and the human being reaches the happiness for which this composition was made—it is mandatory for this species not to let any of these three powers follow the path of excess (الإفراط) or deficiency (التفريط), nor to deviate from the exact middle (حاق الوسط) toward the two sides of increase and decrease. For in that is the departure of a part of the composite from the measure taken from it in the establishment of the original composition, and in that is the departure of the composite from being that [specific] composite, the necessary result of which is the nullification of the goal of the composition, which is the happiness of the species.

The limit of moderation (الاعتدال) in the appetitive power—which is its use as is appropriate in quantity and quality—is called temperance (عفة). The two sides in excess and deficiency are greed (الشره) and apathy (الخمود). The limit of moderation in the irascible power is courage (الشجاعة), and the two sides are recklessness (التهور) and cowardice (الجبن). The limit of moderation in the reflective power is called wisdom (حكمة), and the two sides are cunning (الجربزة) and dullness (البلادة). From the gathering of these traits in the soul, a fourth trait arises which is like the temperament (المزاج) of the mixture; it is called justice (عدالة). It is giving every possessor of a right among the powers its due, and placing it in its proper position. The two sides within it are oppression (الظلم) and being oppressed (الانظلام).

These are the foundations of virtuous ethics: temperance, courage, wisdom, and justice. Each of them has branches arising from them and returning, upon analysis, to them. Their relation to the aforementioned foundations is like the relation of the species to the genus—such as generosity and liberality, contentment and gratitude, patience and magnanimity, boldness and modesty, zeal and sincerity, dignity and humility, and others. These are the branches of virtuous ethics recorded in the books of ethics. (Here is a tree illustrating their foundations and the branching of their derivatives).

[Image: Tree of Virtuous Ethics]

The science of ethics explains the limit of each one of them and distinguishes them from their two sides in excess and deficiency. Then it explains that they are good and beautiful, and then points to the manner of adopting them as a trait (ملكة) in the soul through the two paths of knowledge and action—meaning, the conviction that they are good and beautiful, and the repetition of acting upon them until they become a firmly rooted disposition (هيئة راسخة) in the soul.

An example of this is to say: Cowardice only arises from the empowerment of fear over the soul. Fear is only regarding a matter whose occurrence and non-occurrence are possible. In that which has equal possibilities, it is base to prefer one side over the other without a preponderant factor (مرجح). A rational person should not do that; therefore, a human being should not fear.

When a human being instills this saying into themselves and then repeats the act of venturing into and entering fearsome and perilous situations, the vice of fear vanishes from them. Such is the case with other vices and virtues.

![شجرة الأخلاق الفاضلة](data:image/png;base64,)


This is what the first path necessitates according to what was previously explained. Its summary is the reform of the soul and the moderation of its traits for the purpose of attaining a praiseworthy quality and beautiful commendation.

Similar to it is what the second path necessitates—which is the path of the prophets and the masters of the divine laws. The difference is only in terms of the purpose and the goal. For the goal of ethical perfection in the first path is the virtue praised by people and their beautiful commendation. Its goal in the second path is the true happiness of the human being—which is the perfection of faith in Allah and His signs, and the reward of the Hereafter. This is a happiness and perfection in reality, not only in the eyes of people. Nevertheless, the two paths share the fact that the ultimate goal and purpose within them is human virtue from the perspective of action.

As for the third path previously explained, it differs from the first two in that the purpose within it is seeking the face of Allah (ابتغاء وجه الله), not the acquisition of human virtue. Therefore, the objectives within it may differ from those in the first two paths; perhaps ethical moderation in it is not the same moderation found in them, and so on.

The explanation of this is that when the servant's faith begins to intensify and increase, his soul is drawn toward reflection upon his Lord and the presence of His Beautiful Names and His attributes of beauty, which are sanctified from deficiency and blemish. His soul continues to increase in attraction and progresses in watchfulness (مراقبة) until he comes to worship Allah as if he sees Him, and that his Lord sees him. He manifests to him in the loci of attraction, watchfulness, and love. Thus, love begins to intensify, for the human being is created with an innate disposition (مفطور) toward the love of the beautiful. The Exalted has said: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِّلَّهِ** — 'but those who have faith are more ardent in their love of Allah' [2:165]. He begins to follow the Messenger in all his movements and stillness, because the love of a thing necessitates the love of its effects, and the Messenger is among His effects and signs, just as the world is also among His effects and signs, the Exalted.

This love continues to intensify and intensify until he is cut off from everything else for His sake. He loves none but his Lord, and his heart submits to none but His face. For this servant encounters nothing, and comes across nothing that possesses any beauty or goodness, except that he finds what it possesses to be a sample (انموذج) that recounts the inexhaustible perfection, the infinite beauty, and the boundless goodness that is with Him. To Him belongs all goodness, beauty, perfection, and splendor. Everything that belonged to another belongs to Him, because everything other than Him is a sign (آية) for Him, having no independent reality (نفسية) of its own; it is only a narrative that recounts its Owner. This servant has been overcome by the authority of love over his heart, and it continues to overcome him. He looks at nothing except because it is a sign among the signs of his Lord. In short, his love is severed from everything else and directed toward his Lord; he loves nothing except for Allah, the Glorified, and in Allah, the Glorified.

At that moment, the nature of his perception (إدراكه) and action (عمله) undergoes a transformation; he no longer sees anything without seeing Allah, the Glorified, before it and with it. Things lose their status of independence (الاستقلال) in his view. Thus, the forms of knowledge and perception he possesses are unlike those held by other people, for they look at everything from behind the veil of independence, whereas he does not. This is from the perspective of knowledge (العلم).

The same applies to the perspective of action (العمل); since he loves only for the sake of Allah, he desires nothing except for Allah and seeking His Noble Face (وجهه الكريم). He does not seek, intend, hope, fear, choose, abandon, despair, feel lonely, feel satisfied, or become angry except for Allah and in Allah. Consequently, his objectives differ from the objectives of other people, and the ultimate goal of his actions is transformed. Until this moment, he used to choose an action and intend perfection (الكمال) because it was a human virtue (فضيلة إنسانية), and he would avoid an action or a trait because it was a human vice (رذيلة إنسانية). But now, he only desires the Face of his Lord; he has no concern for virtue or vice, no preoccupation with fair praise or honorable mention, and no regard for this world, the hereafter, paradise, or hell. His sole concern is his Lord, his provision is the humility of his servitude (ذل عبوديته), and his guide is his love (حبه).

*The narrations of passion were told to me by longing,*
*With their chains of transmission from the neighbors of the Unique Knowledge.*
*The passing breeze spoke to me from the zephyr,*
*From the lush trees, from the valley of the open space, from my Lord of Najd.*

This explanation we have provided—though we have preferred brevity and conciseness—will, if you reflect upon it deeply, be found sufficient for the objective. It becomes clear that in this third path, the very subject of virtue (الفضيلة) and vice (الرذيلة) is elevated, and the goal and purpose—namely, human virtue—is replaced by a single purpose: the Face of Allah. Sometimes the perspective of this path differs from others, such that what is counted as a virtue in another path becomes a vice in this one, and vice versa.

One point remains: there is another theory in ethics (الأخلاق) that differs from what has preceded and is sometimes considered a separate path. It posits that ethics vary in their principles and branches according to the variation of civil societies (الاجتماعات المدنية), due to the variation in beauty (الحسن) — *the quality of being morally good or fitting* — and ugliness (القبح) — *the quality of being morally bad or unfitting* — without tracing back to a fixed, self-sustaining principle. It has been claimed that this is a result of the well-known theory of evolution and perfection in matter (نظرية التحول والتكامل في المادة).

They say: Human society is the product of all existential needs that man seeks to fulfill through social life (الاجتماع). He uses this as a means to preserve the existence of society, which he views as the preservation of his own individual existence. Since nature is governed by the law of transformation and perfection, society is also inherently changing, moving at every moment toward what is more perfect and advanced. Beauty (الحسن) and ugliness (القبح)—which are the conformity or non-conformity of an action to the goal of society, i.e., perfection—have no meaning in remaining in a single state or being frozen in a unique way. Thus, there is no absolute beauty and no absolute ugliness; rather, they are always relative (نسبيان), differing according to societies in terms of place and time. If beauty and ugliness are relative and changing, then ethics must change, and virtues and vices must be transformed. From this, it is concluded that ethics follow the national objective (المرام القومي), which is the means to attain civil perfection and the social goal, because beauty and ugliness follow that. Whatever leads to progress and the attainment of the goal is a virtue and possesses beauty, and whatever calls for stagnation or regression is a vice. Based on this, perhaps lying, slander, indecency, wretchedness, cruelty, theft, and impudence could be beautiful and virtuous if they occur on the path of the social objective, while honesty, chastity, and mercy could be an ugly vice if they lead to deprivation of the desired goal. This is the summary of this strange theory adopted by the materialist socialists. This theory is not modern, as they claim; for the Cynics (الكلبيون) among the ancient Greeks—as is reported—followed this path, as did the Mazdakites (المزدكيون) (the followers of Mazdak who appeared in Iran during the era of Khosrow and called for socialism), whose practice was based on this, and it is also known among some primitive tribes in Africa and elsewhere.

In any case, it is a corrupt path, and the argument established for this theory is corrupt in both its structure (البناء) and its foundation (المبنى).

To clarify: We find that every existing thing among these external, concrete entities (الموجودات العينية الخارجية) possesses an identity (شخصية) that accompanies it. This identity necessitates that the entity, because of it, is not identical to another entity and remains distinct in its existence. Just as the existence of Zayd is accompanied by an identity and a type of unity (وحدة) that makes it impossible for him to be identical to ‘Amr; Zayd is one individual and ‘Amr is another, and they are two individuals, not one. This is a reality beyond doubt (and this is different from our saying: the world of matter is an existent with a single personal reality, so the matter should not be confused).

The result of this is: External existence (الوجود الخارجي) is identical to identity (الشخصية). However, mental concepts (المفاهيم الذهنية) differ from external existents in this ruling; for the intellect allows a meaning, whatever it may be, to apply to more than one instance (مصداق), such as the concept of "man," the concept of "tall man," or the concept of "this man standing before us." As for the logicians' division of concepts into universal (الكلي) and particular (الجزئي), and their division of the particular into relative (الإضافي) and real (الحقيقي), it is merely a division based on relation and proportion (الإضافة والنسبة)—either the relation of one concept to another or its relation to the external world. This attribute found in concepts—namely, the possibility of applying to more than one—is what we sometimes call "absoluteness" (الإطلاق), just as we call its opposite "identity" (الشخصية) or "unity" (الوحدة).

Furthermore, the external existent (by which we specifically mean the material existent) is subject to the law of change and general motion (الحركة العمومية). It necessarily possesses extension (امتداد) and is divisible into limits and segments (قطعات), each segment differing from the segment that preceded or followed it. Nevertheless, they are linked by their existence; for if it were not so, the meaning of change and transformation (التبدل) would not be true. If one thing were to vanish entirely and another were to come into being entirely, that would not be the transformation of the former into the latter. Rather, the transformation that accompanies every motion is only realized by the existence of a common factor (قدر مشترك) in both states.

From this, it appears that motion (الحركة) is a single thing in its identity, which becomes multiple according to its relation to the limits. Thus, by every relation, a segment is determined that differs from the other segments. As for the motion itself, it is a single, personal flow and current. We may call this attribute in motion "absoluteness" (إطلاقاً) in contrast to the relations it has to each specific limit. Thus, we say "absolute motion" (الحركة المطلقة) meaning disregarding its relation to the limits. From this, it appears that "absolute" in the second sense is a real thing existing externally, unlike "absolute" in the first sense, for absoluteness in that sense is a mental attribute of a mental existent.

Furthermore, we do not doubt that man is a natural existent (موجود طبيعي) possessing individuals, rulings, and properties. What creation (الخلقة) brings into existence is the individual (الفرد) among the individuals of man, not the sum of individuals—that is, human society (الاجتماع الإنساني). However, when creation sensed the deficiency of his existence and his need for perfections that he cannot achieve alone, it equipped him with tools and powers suited to his quest for perfection within the context of society and among socialized individuals. Thus, the nature of the individual human (طبيعة الإنسان الفرد) is intended by creation primarily and essentially (أولاً وبالذات), while society is intended secondarily and subordinately (ثانياً وبالتبع).

As for the reality of man's affair with this society, which human nature necessitates and moves toward (if it is correct to apply "necessitation," "causality," and "motion" to the case of society in reality): the individual human is a single personal existent in the sense of identity and unity mentioned before. He is, nevertheless, in a state of motion, transforming and changing toward perfection. Hence, every segment of his changing existence is different from other segments, yet he possesses a flowing, absolute nature (طبيعة سيالة مطلقة) preserved throughout the stages of changes as a single personal entity. This nature existing in the individual is preserved through procreation, reproduction, and the derivation of one individual from another—this is what we express as the "specific nature" (الطبيعة النوعية). It is preserved through individuals even if they change or are subject to corruption and generation (الفساد والكون), by the same explanation given regarding the individual nature. Thus, the personal nature (الطبيعة الشخصية) exists and is directed toward individual perfection, and the specific nature exists as an absolute and is directed toward perfection.

This specific perfection (الاستكمال النوعي) undoubtedly exists and is realized in the system of nature. It is what we rely upon when we say: the human species, for example, is directed toward perfection, and man today is more perfect in existence than the primitive man. The same applies to what the hypothesis of the evolution of species dictates. If there were no external specific nature preserved in individuals or species, for example, this talk would be nothing but poetic speech.

The discussion regarding the "personal society" (الاجتماع الشخصي) existing among the individuals of a people, or in an era, or in an environment, and the "specific society" (نوع الاجتماع) existing within the human species—which continues with its continuity and transforms with its transformation (if it were correct that society, like the socialized human, is an external state of an external nature!)—is analogous to the discussion of the personal and specific nature of man in terms of restriction (التقييد) and absoluteness (الإطلاق).

Thus, society is moving and changing with the motion and change of man. It has a unity from the beginning of the motion to its destination through an absolute existence. This single changing entity, by means of its relation and addition to each specific limit, becomes segment by segment, and each segment is a single individual among the individuals of society. The individuals of society are based in their existence on the individuals of man, just as absolute society in the sense mentioned is based on the absolute human nature. For the ruling of the individual is an individual ruling, and the ruling of the absolute is an absolute ruling (not a universal ruling, for we do not mean conceptual absoluteness, so do not be heedless). We do not doubt that the individual human, being one, has a single ruling that remains with his survival, though it changes with partial changes following the changes occurring to its subject, which is man. Among the rulings of the natural man is that he nourishes himself, acts by will, senses, and thinks—this exists with man and remains with his survival, even if it changes according to his own internal changes. The same applies to the rulings of the absolute man existing through the existence of his individuals.

Since society is one of the rulings and properties of human nature, absolute society (by which we mean the continuous society brought about by the continuous human nature from the time the individual man existed until our day) is one of the properties of the absolute human species, existing with it and remaining with its survival. The rulings of society that it brought about and necessitated are with society, existing with its existence and remaining with its survival, even if they change with partial changes while the principle, like their type, is preserved. At that point, it is correct for us to say: there are social rulings that remain unchanged, such as the existence of absolute beauty and ugliness, just as absolute society itself is so—meaning that society does not turn into something other than society, like isolation, even if a specific society changes into another specific one. Absolute and specific beauty are exactly like absolute and specific society.

Furthermore, we see that the individual human, in his existence and survival, needs perfections and benefits that he must attract and incorporate into himself. The proof of this obligation (الوجوب) is his need in various aspects of his existence and creation's equipping him with what enables him to do so, such as the digestive system and the reproductive system, for example. Thus, man must pursue this, and he certainly cannot fail to do so through negligence (التفريط), for that would contradict the proof of obligation we mentioned. Nor can he pursue any area of need in a way that exceeds what is necessary through excess (الإفراط), such as eating until he dies, or becomes ill, or is disabled from his other active powers. Rather, he must be moderate (يتوسط) in seeking every perfection or benefit. This moderation is chastity (العفة), and its two extremes are greed (الشره) and apathy (الخمود). Similarly, we see the individual in his existence and survival situated between deficiencies, opposites, and harms to his existence which he must repel. The proof for this is the need and the equipment within himself. Thus, he must resist and defend with the appropriate moderation, without excess that contradicts his other equipment, or negligence that contradicts the related need and equipment. This moderation is courage (الشجاعة), and its two extremes are recklessness (التهور) and cowardice (الجبن). A similar discussion applies to knowledge (العلم) and its two opposites, namely cunning (الجربزة) and stupidity (البلادة), and to justice (العدالة) and its two opposites, which are oppression (الظلم) and suffering oppression (الانظلام).

These are four traits and virtues necessitated by the individual nature equipped with its tools: chastity (العفة), courage (الشجاعة), wisdom (الحكمة), and justice (العدالة). They are all beautiful (حسنة) because the meaning of beauty is suitability to the goal, perfection, and happiness of a thing. They are all suitable and appropriate for the happiness of the individual by the proof mentioned previously. Their opposites are ugly vices (رذائل قبيحة). If the individual human, by his nature and in himself, possesses this description, then he also possesses it within the context of society. How could society—which is one of the rulings of this nature—nullify its other existential rulings? Would this be anything other than a contradiction within a single nature? The reality of society is nothing but the cooperation of individuals in facilitating the path toward the perfection of their natures and reaching the goal of their aspirations.

If the individual human in himself and within the context of society is thus described, then the human species in its specific society is also like that. Thus, the human species in its society achieves perfection through defense to the extent that does not corrupt society, through the acquisition of benefits to the extent that does not corrupt society, through knowledge to the extent that does not corrupt society, and through social justice—which is giving every possessor of a right his right and his reaching the portion that befits him without oppression or suffering oppression. All these four traits are virtues by the ruling of absolute society; human society dictates their absolute beauty and considers their opposites to be vices and dictates their ugliness.

It has become clear through this explanation: that in continuous human society, there is a beauty and an ugliness from which it is never devoid, and that the four principles of ethics are always beautiful virtues, and their opposites are always ugly vices. Social human nature dictates this. If the matter regarding the principles is of this sort, then the branches that lead back to them upon analysis are also subject to this acceptance, even if some variation occurs in their instances (مصداق) in terms of application, as we shall point out.

Once you understand what we have mentioned, the reason for the collapse of what we quoted from their claims regarding ethics will become apparent to you. Here is the explanation:

As for their saying: "Absolute beauty and ugliness do not exist; rather, what exists is relative beauty and ugliness, which change and vary according to regions, times, and societies," this is a fallacy arising from the confusion between conceptual absoluteness (الإطلاق المفهومي) in the sense of universality (الكلية) and existential absoluteness (الإطلاق الوجودي) in the sense of continuity of existence. Absolute, universal beauty and ugliness do not exist externally with the attribute of universality and absoluteness, but they are not what necessitate the result we intend. As for absolute, continuous beauty and ugliness in the sense of their continuity as rulings of society as long as society continues with the continuity of nature, they do exist. For the goal of society is the happiness of the species, and it is impossible for all possible and hypothetical actions to conform to society however it is conceived. Thus, there are always actions that conform and actions that conflict; therefore, there is always beauty and ugliness.

Accordingly, how can any society be conceived, however it may be, without its members believing that it is obligatory to give every possessor of a right his right, or that seeking benefits to the appropriate extent is obligatory, or that defending the interests of society to the appropriate extent is necessary, or that the knowledge by which human benefits are distinguished from others is a beautiful virtue? These are justice, chastity, courage, and wisdom, which we mentioned that human society, however conceived, cannot but judge as beautiful and as human virtues. Similarly, how is it possible for a society not to judge the necessity of restraint and being affected by the manifestation of the foul and hideous? This is modesty (الحياء), which is a branch of chastity. Or not to judge the necessity of anger and the change of soul at the violation of sanctities and the erosion of rights? This is zeal (الغيرة), which is a branch of courage. Or not to judge the necessity of restricting oneself to the social rights one possesses? This is contentment (القناعة). Or not to judge the necessity of preserving the self in its social position without trampling on people or belittling them through arrogance and wrongful transgression? This is humility (التواضع). And so it is for every single one of the branches of the virtues.

As for what they claim regarding the variation of views in different societies concerning specific virtues, and a single trait becoming a virtue for one people and a vice for another in particular examples, this is not due to a variation in the view regarding the social ruling—such that one people believes in the obligation of following a beautiful virtue and others do not—but rather due to the variation in the application of the ruling to the instance (المصداق).

For example, the societies that were managed by autocratic governments used to see for the throne of the king complete choice to do as he pleased and rule as he wished. This was not due to their poor opinion of justice, but because of their belief that this was among the rights of sovereignty and kingship. Thus, it was not oppression from the position of sovereignty, but rather the fulfillment of his rightful rights according to their claim.

Another example: knowledge was once considered a disgrace for kings in some societies, as is recounted about the French nation in the Middle Ages. This was not because they belittled the virtue of knowledge, but because of their claim that knowledge of politics and the arts of governing conflicted with sovereign preoccupations.

Another example: the chastity of women in the sense of guarding the private parts from other than the husband, as well as modesty in women, and zeal in their men, as well as a number of virtues like contentment and humility, are traits whose virtue is not acknowledged in some societies. However, this is because their specific society does not consider them instances of chastity, modesty, zeal, contentment, and humility—not because these virtues are not virtues to them. The proof for this is the existence of their principle among them; for they praise the chastity of a ruler in his ruling and a judge in his judgment, they praise modesty in not violating the laws, they praise zeal in defending independence, civilization, and all their sanctities, they praise contentment with the rights the law has assigned to them, and they praise humility toward their leaders and guides in society.

As for their saying that ethics in their beauty revolve around their conformity to the goal of the social objective (المرام الاجتماعي), and their concluding this from the fact that their beauty revolves around conformity to the goal of society, there is a clear fallacy in this. For what is meant by "society" (الاجتماع) is the form resulting from the operation of the sum of laws that nature has established among socialized individuals, which must lead to their happiness were it not for the disruption of its order and flow; it necessarily has rulings of beauty, ugliness, virtue, and vice. What is meant by "objective" (المرام) is the sum of hypotheses set forth to create a society in a new form by imposing them upon socialized individuals. That is, society and the social objective are distinct in terms of actuality and potentiality, and realization and the hypothesis of realization. How then can the ruling of one become identical to the ruling of the other? And how can beauty, ugliness, virtue, and vice—which the general society has determined by the necessity of human nature—be transformed into what is judged by an objective that is nothing but a hypothesis from a hypothesizer?

If it were said: general natural society has no ruling of its own, but the ruling belongs to the objective—especially if it is a hypothesis suited to the happiness of individuals—the previous discussion regarding beauty, ugliness, virtue, and vice would return, and that they ultimately trace back to a continuous necessity from nature.

Moreover, there is another problem here: if beauty, ugliness, and other social rulings—which are what social arguments rely upon and from which deductions are formed—were dependent on the objective, and it is possible, indeed a reality, that different, contradictory, and distinct objectives are realized, this would lead to the disappearance of the common argument accepted by all societies. Progress and success would then belong only to power and coercion. How can it be said that human nature led its individuals to a social life where there is no understanding between its parts and no ruling that unites them except a ruling that nullifies society itself? Is this not a hideous contradiction in the ruling of nature and its existential necessity?

## (Another Narrative Discussion)

### On Miscellaneous Matters Related to What Preceded

From al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "A man came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'I am desirous and energetic for Jihad.' He said: 'Then perform Jihad in the way of Allah, for if you are killed, you will be alive with Allah, provided for; and if you die, your reward has already fallen upon Allah...'" (the hadith).

His saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "And if you die..." etc., is an allusion to the saying of the Exalted: **وَمَن يَخْرُجْ مِن بَيْتِهِ مُهَاجِرًا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ الْمَوْتُ فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللَّهِ** — "And whoever leaves his home migrating toward Allah and His Apostle, and then death overtakes him, his reward has already fallen on Allah" (An-Nisa: 100). In this, there is an indication that going out for Jihad is a migration (مهاجرة) toward Allah and His Messenger.

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the Prophet Ishmael, whom Allah, the Glorified, named "Truthful to the Promise" (صادق الوعد): He (peace be upon him) said: "He was only named Truthful to the Promise because he promised a man at a place and waited for him at that place for a year; so Allah (عز وجل) named him Truthful to the Promise. Then the man came to him after that time, and Ishmael said to him: 'I have not ceased waiting for you...'" (the hadith).

**I say:** This is a matter that the ordinary intellect (العقل العادي) might judge as deviating from the path of moderation, yet Allah, the Glorified, made it a merit for him (peace be upon him), such that He magnified his status and raised his mention by His saying: **وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صَادِقَ الْوَعْدِ وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُ بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ وَكَانَ عِندَ رَبِّهِ مَرْضِيًّا** — "And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed he was true to his promise, and he was an apostle, a prophet. He used to bid his family to prayer and to maintain the alms-tax, and he was pleasing to his Lord" (Maryam: 54-55). This is only because the scale (الميزان) by which this action was weighed is different from the scale in the hand of the ordinary intellect. For the ordinary intellect has a training through its own management, while Allah, the Glorified, has a training for His friends (أولياء) through His support, and the word of Allah is the highest. Instances of this case are many, narrated and transmitted from the Prophet, the Imams, and the friends of Allah.

**If you say:** How can the Law (الشرع) differ from the intellect (العقل) in matters where the intellect has a path?

**I say:** As for the ruling of the intellect in what it has a path toward, it is in its proper place. However, it requires a subject (موضوع) upon which its ruling falls. You have learned in what preceded that such sciences in the third path we mentioned do not leave for the intellect a subject to judge in or upon. This is the path of divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية). It appears that the Prophet Ishmael (peace be upon him) had made an absolute statement in his promise by saying: "I will wait for you here until you return to me," then he committed himself to the absoluteness of his word to protect his soul from breaking the covenant and lying in the promise, and to preserve what Allah had cast into his heart and made flow upon his tongue. A similar thing has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he was at the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) and one of his companions promised to return to him, and the Prophet promised to wait for him until he returned. The man went about his business and did not return, so the Prophet waited for him for three days in the place he had promised until the man passed by him after the three days while he was sitting waiting, the man having forgotten the promise (the hadith).

In *al-Khasa'is* by al-Sayyid al-Radi, from Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said—having heard a man saying: "Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return"—: "O you! Our saying 'Indeed we belong to Allah' is an acknowledgment from us of His ownership (الملك), and 'Indeed to Him we shall return' is an acknowledgment from us of our perishing (الهلاك)."

**I say:** Its meaning has become clear through what preceded, and it was narrated in *al-Kafi* in detail.

In *al-Kafi*: from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar and ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Allah (عز وجل) said: I have made the world a loan (قرضاً) among My servants; so whoever gives Me a loan in it, I give him for every one ten to seven hundred fold. And whoever does not give Me a loan, and I take something from him by force (قسراً), I give him three traits which, if I gave one of them to My angels, they would be pleased with Me for it.'" Then Abu ‘Abdullah said: "The saying of Allah: **الَّذِينَ إِذَا أَصَابَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالُوا إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ رَاجِعُونَ ، أُولَئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَاتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ** — 'those who, when an affliction visits them, say, "Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return." It is they who receive the blessings from their Lord' (Al-Baqarah: 156-157). This is one of the three traits. **وَرَحْمَةٌ** — 'and [His] mercy' is the second. **وَأُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْمُهْتَدُونَ** — 'and it is they who are the [rightly] guided' is the third." Then Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him) said: "This is for the one from whom Allah takes something by force."

**I say:** The narration is reported through other similar chains.

In *al-Ma'ani*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Prayer (الصلوة) from Allah is mercy (رحمة), from the angels it is purification (التزكية), and from people it is supplication (دعاء)."

**I say:** There are several other narrations with this meaning. There is an apparent conflict between this narration and the one before it, as the previous narration counts "blessings" (الصلوات) as distinct from "mercy" (الرحمة), which is supported by the literal wording of His saying: **عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَاتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ** — "receive the blessings from their Lord and [His] mercy." This narration, however, counts it as mercy. The conflict is resolved by referring to the explanation provided previously.
